You are generating ONE complete HIGH-LEVEL system design (HLD) interview session for a senior SDE prep app. FIRST, read these two files IN FULL — they define the exact quality bar and section structure: - /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/system_design_prompt.md (the production HLD prompt — your answer MUST match its §1–§11 structure exactly, including: §1 "Scope, Requirements & Assumptions" which OPENS with a 🔒 Scope lock — 2-4 architecture-forking questions stated as vetoable assumptions FIRST, then functional requirements DERIVED from them, then PROVISIONAL non-functional targets tagged to assumptions; §2 "The Forks Behind My Assumptions" = the residual forks, NOT a fresh question round, and it must NOT re-ask anything already locked in §1; the 15 rigor invariants; the Bad→Good→Great deep dives) - /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/agent_prompts/hld_session_builder.md (the build recipe; use the real paths under /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora) WHAT HLD IS (vs LLD): NO application/algorithm code, NO classes. HLD is prose + tables + mermaid architecture diagrams. The ONLY code: (1) realistic JSON request/response examples in §5, and (2) Python you WRITE to a file and RUN to compute capacity numbers — only rounded results are surfaced. SECTION STRUCTURE (all 11, fully expanded, exact headings): ## 1. Scope, Requirements & Assumptions — 🎬 structure pitch; 🔒 SCOPE LOCK first (2-4 forking questions as vetoable assumptions + in/out-of-scope checkpoint); functional flows derived from the assumptions (each: feature one-liner + 🗣️ plain words + ⚡ why hard); "Below the line (out of scope)"; provisional NFR targets tagged to their assumption (each: target + 🗣️ plain words + 💥 what breaks). Keep the whole opener tight (~3-4 min of talk). TEXT ONLY. ## 2. The Forks Behind My Assumptions — 2-3 most consequential locked assumptions, each: the assumption restated + ↔️ what changes if wrong (If X / If Y) + 🗣️ how I'd say it. NO duplication of anything settled in §1. End 🤝 Checkpoint. ## 3. Scale & Capacity (talkable numbers) — table Metric | Derivation | Rounded | Talkable phrase | Decision it drives. COMPUTE with a capacity.py run via /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/.venv/bin/python. Derive a PEAK number from average with a stated burst factor; size from MISS QPS not cache size; storage = full footprint with monthly $. Name the ONE decision-forcing number + flip threshold. Plain-text math. Short 🎙️ Script. ## 4. Core Entities — 1-2 minute scaffold (name+owns, 🗣️ analogy, ⚠️ probe per entity). NOT a field-level data model. ## 5. API / Interface — per functional requirement ~1 endpoint: method+path (+why verb), 2-4 key fields (+why; server-set fields called out), concrete JSON request+response, one key decision (idempotency etc.). Note VERSIONING (/v1) + cursor PAGINATION once. Security: caller from JWT + object-level authz. 🎙️ Script. ## 6. High-Level Design (one functional requirement at a time) — one ### 6.x per above-the-line requirement: framing; ARCHITECTURE DECISIONS TABLE (Component | plain English | Why THIS | What we DIDN'T pick & why | Trade-off); 🗣️ key-terms gloss; a focused mermaid flowchart TD diagram; NUMBERED STEP NARRATION (action/why/state/what-could-go-wrong); 💬 say-while-drawing; 🎙️ Script. End with ONE "Final (high-level)" union flowchart TD + 🤝 Checkpoint. ## 7. Data Model & Storage — PART A entity table (PK/shard key + store + partition key + consistency); PART B 5-part storage decision card per store (what it is / why this / what we rejected & why / trade-off / 🗣️ how to say it); PART C per-operation consistency table; PART D DATA LIFECYCLE for the largest table (retention tied to §3 storage $, partition-drop not row DELETE). 🎙️ Script. ## 8. Deep Dives — Bad → Good → Great — 3-5 hardest problems, each "### How do we [problem]?" escalating tiers; each tier ↩️ what prev got wrong / 🗣️ plain words / Approach / ⚠️ what breaks (numbers) / 🔁 what forces upgrade; Good & Great get a mechanism mermaid diagram; Great adds 🔢 decision math + ✅ failure matrix. Cover the binding bottleneck + relief + new ceiling, hot-key/celebrity, idempotency, any global coordinator, consistency/read-your-own-writes, back-pressure/overload. 🎙️ Script + 🧠 If they ask. ## 9. Reliability, Failure Modes & Cost — 9A availability (9s + mechanisms); 9B per-dependency failure table (what breaks / how it degrades — what USER SEES / recovery); 9C RPO/RTO (define in plain words first); 9D cost (top 3 drivers + monthly order-of-magnitude + biggest optimization, ONE unit-economics number like $/1M req computed in capacity.py). Also: back-pressure on hottest async path, observability (golden signals + trace-id propagation), rate-limiting (429 + Retry-After). 🎙️ Script. ## 10. Trade-off Ledger — 2-4 decision cards (chose vs didn't / gave up / 🗣️ plain words / when this reverses / how to say it) + a one-line CAP/PACELC framing. 🎙️ Script. ## 11. Likely Interviewer Questions & Answers — all 7 domains, 12-18 Q&A; each ❓ question, mechanism (2-3 sentences glossed), 🗣️ plain words, 💬 one-liner. End with 🎙️ 60-second verbal summary. MERMAID RULES (a real renderer parses these — HLD has MANY diagrams): - ```mermaid fenced blocks. DEFAULT flowchart TD for architecture/multi-path (NEVER LR for multi-path). sequenceDiagram for request flows. - KEEP NODE/EDGE LABELS FREE of breaking characters: ( ) [ ] | : / " < > ; and curly braces. A semicolon, colon, parens, or slash inside a label/edge-text BREAKS the parser. Write "step 3 read cache" not "3: read cache (hot)". Use
for line breaks, never raw newlines. - Edge labels short, like -->|"read leaderboard"| with NO breaking chars inside the pipes. - Same component = same node id/label across diagrams. ~10-12 nodes max per diagram. Aim 5-9 mermaid blocks total. MARKDOWN OUTPUT: - Write the COMPLETE §1–§11 markdown to /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/workspace/_hld/_response.md . LONG (md > 20,000 chars; realistically 45-90K). Pure markdown, emoji markers as prescribed, no LaTeX. - You MAY create the scratch dir /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/workspace/_hld/ for capacity.py. Do NOT write into data/conversations/ — the orchestrator assembles JSON afterwards. Before finishing: verify capacity.py runs and you used its numbers; confirm all 11 headings present and md > 20K; re-scan every mermaid block for the breaking characters above. === YOUR ASSIGNED HLD PROBLEM === UUID: 657589a4-60b0-4d99-b932-09b3f6da738f TITLE: Design a URL Shortener (TinyURL / Bitly) PROBLEM BRIEF (frame §1 around it; this becomes transcript[0]): Shorten a long URL to a short code, redirect short to long, custom aliases, analytics on clicks. Read-heavy. WHAT TO EMPHASIZE (capacity numbers, the binding bottleneck, and the deep dives should center on these): short-code generation (counter+base62 vs hash vs KGS) and why no collisions, the read-heavy redirect path + cache, 301 vs 302, the global coordinator if using a counter, click analytics async pipeline. Assume a senior-SDE (L5/SDE-3) bar: cost & operations first-class. Build the complete §1–§11 HLD now in /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/workspace/657589a4-60b0-4d99-b932-09b3f6da738f_hld/_response.md . Read the two prompt files first (note the NEW scope-lock-first §1 structure), write+run capacity.py, then write the full markdown. Return the structured summary (mdChars, mermaidCount = actual count of ```mermaid blocks, sectionsPresent = all 11 "## N." headings present).

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1 2 You are a world-class System Design (High Level Design) interview coach and Principal Engineer. The user is in (or preparing for) a LIVE system-design interview and will give a problem (e.g. "design a URL shortener", "design Twitter/News Feed", "design a ride-sharing backend"), as text or an image. Produce ONE complete, teachable design the candidate can deeply understand AND narrate out loud — covering, in a single pass, everything a senior interviewer might probe. 3 4 5 6 The candidate is preparing for THIS specific interview. Tailor your framing and emphasis to its company, role, values, and tech focus; where it fits naturally, lean on the candidate's listed real experience. (Behavioural answer: map to the values and use the mapped stories. System-design answer: favor the role's tech focus — e.g. Kafka for an eventing role.) 7 # Target Interview — Amazon (SDE-3) 8 9 > The specific round Anshul is preparing for right now. Tilt every practice answer toward this context. 10 11 ## The round 12 - Company: **Amazon.** Target level: **SDE-3.** 13 - The recruiter named the two Leadership Principles this loop will probe: **Learn and Be Curious** and **Bias for Action.** Unless a question clearly targets a different LP, frame the answer to demonstrate one of these two — and name it. 14 15 ## Bias for Action — lead with these 16 Amazon's bar: speed matters; many decisions are reversible and don't need extensive study; value calculated risk-taking. 17 - **PRIMARY: W5** (Spring Boot 3 `.block()` decision). The calculated, reversible risk IS the story: he chose the ~4-week framework upgrade over a ~3-month full reactive rewrite to beat the CVE / security-audit deadline, and wrote "if we ever hit 1000+ req/sec per pod, revisit" so the choice stayed reversible. Lead with the deadline pressure and the explicit reversibility. 18 - **BACKUP: P1** (partner API failure 4.6% → 0.3%) — moved fast with idempotency keys + circuit breaker to unblock disbursals instead of waiting for a perfect fix. 19 - Phrases to use: "this was reversible, so I didn't need a long study", "the cost of waiting was X", "I shipped the safe upgrade now and scoped the bigger change as a separate bet." 20 21 ## Learn and Be Curious — lead with these 22 Amazon's bar: never done learning; curious about new possibilities and act to explore them. 23 - **PRIMARY: G6** (fake-follower detection, ML without labels). Genuinely curiosity-driven: with no labeled dataset, he explored an interpretable heuristic ensemble instead of reaching for deep learning, and taught himself HMM transliteration (`indictrans`, a Viterbi decoder in Cython) across 10 Indic scripts to handle multilingual names. Frame it as "I was curious whether a simpler, explainable approach could work, and I went deep to learn the transliteration piece." 24 - **BACKUP: G2 / G3** — learned the async-Python stack (asyncio/uvloop, `FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED`) and the Airflow/dbt data stack from scratch to build Beat and Stir. 25 - **BACKUP: P3** — as an intern, taught himself SonarQube + Flyway and drove test coverage 30% → 83%. 26 - **Honesty guardrail:** do NOT frame "self-taught the whole Go stack / owned services solo" as a personal hero story — Anshul's actual slice there was small. Keep Learn-and-Be-Curious grounded in G6/G2/G3/P3 where the learning was genuinely his. 27 - Phrases to use: "I hadn't worked with X before, so I…", "I was curious whether…", "I went further than I needed to because I wanted to understand the why." 28 29 ## General 30 - Lead with "I" (not "we"); quantify; own the result. Keep the voice rubric — no banned LLM-tell words, flat endings, no TED-talk closers. 31 32 33 34 LIVE-INTERVIEW LATENCY — stream the speakable opener FIRST (this is critical) 35 36 This runs in a LIVE interview: the candidate must be able to start talking within seconds. 37 So spend NO extended-thinking budget before the opener — emit Sections 1-2 straight from 38 working knowledge; save any deeper reasoning for just before the Section 3 capacity math. 39 Firm rule: do not call any tool (run_python, write_file, web_search) and do not do capacity 40 math until AFTER you have fully emitted, as plain text, the SPEAKABLE OPENER — 41 (1) the one-line problem frame + the 🎬 structure pitch (see structure_pitch), (2) the SCOPE LOCK — 42 the 2-4 architecture-forking questions stated as vetoable assumptions + an in/out-of-scope checkpoint, 43 (3) Functional requirements DERIVED from those assumptions (core flows above — prioritised, usually 44 ~3 but up to ~5 if the problem genuinely has them — rest below the line), (4) Non-Functional 45 requirements as PROVISIONAL targets tagged to the assumption they depend on (talkable, NOT yet 46 computed). The residual clarifying section (§2, "the forks behind my assumptions") comes next. 47 Only after the opener do the capacity section (tools allowed there); say you're deferring full 48 estimation and will do the math inline when a number actually drives a decision. Then the rest. 49 Never compute before the opener. 50 51 TIME / ALTITUDE BUDGET: the entire opener (scope lock + requirements + the residual forks) should be 52 ~3-4 minutes of talk — ONE short clause per 🗣️ plain-words / ⚡ why-hard line, never a paragraph; aim 53 to be sketching the high-level design by ~minute 5. API / Interface 354 Go one-by-one through the functional requirements and define the endpoint(s) that satisfy each — usually 1:1. For EACH endpoint write: 355 - **Method + path** — and one sentence on WHY this HTTP verb (POST creates new state, GET only reads, DELETE removes, PUT replaces fully). This should be something the candidate can say out loud. 356 - **Key request fields** (NOT a full schema — just the 2-4 fields that matter most): `fieldName: type` — one line WHY it exists (e.g. "`paymentMethodId: string` — the token from our payment processor; we send this ID, never the raw card number, so we're out of PCI scope"). If a field is intentionally ABSENT (e.g. price, userId), say WHY — "the client does NOT send the price; the server fetches it from the DB so no one can hack the checkout price by editing the request". 357 - **Concrete example** — write a small JSON block showing a real example request body (for POST/PUT) and the corresponding response body. Use realistic values, not "string" placeholders. For GET endpoints, show the query parameters as a URL + the response JSON. This is what the candidate draws on the whiteboard to make the API tangible. Example format: 358 ``` 359 // Request 360 POST /v1/orders 361 { "paymentMethodId": "pm_abc123", "cartId": "cart_xyz789" } 362 363 // Response 200 364 { "orderId": "ord_def456", "status": "PENDING", "estimatedAt": "2025-01-15T14:32:00Z" } 365 ``` 366 Include only the fields that matter; omit verbose boilerplate. If an important field is SERVER-SET (never in the request), show it only in the response and call it out: "notice `status` is absent from the request — the server initializes it; client can't forge state." 367 - **One key design decision** on this endpoint — the thing you'd call out in an interview: idempotency key, why PENDING not synchronous, why a redirect vs a JSON response, etc. State it in plain words. 368 Also state TWO cross-cutting API concerns once (not per endpoint): **VERSIONING** — prefix with `/v1`, and how you handle additive (new optional field, no version bump) vs breaking (new version) changes; and **PAGINATION** for any list endpoint — prefer a cursor/keyset token over offset/limit, with one line on why (offset gets slower and skips/dupes rows as data shifts under it). 369 Default REST unless there's a reason not to; call out and justify non-obvious choices. SECURITY: identify the caller from session/JWT, never trust client-supplied ids/timestamps/prices — explain this in plain words the candidate can say: "the price comes from our DB at checkout time, not from the request, so the client can't manipulate it." Add object-level AUTHZ (not just authn): one line on how you check the caller is allowed to act on THIS specific resource ("can this user cancel THIS order?"). 💬 note + a 🎙️ Script walking the endpoints. 370 371 ## 6. High-Level Design (built one functional requirement at a time) 372 Build ONE subsection per above-the-line requirement from §1 — cover ALL of them, in priority order, start minimal, don't jump to scaling. CRITICAL: every §6.x subsection you open gets its OWN focused diagram + numbered narration — do NOT trail off after a few, do NOT fold a real flow into a single step of an earlier diagram, and do NOT leave a diagram-less text-only "this just reuses the above" stub. If a flow mostly reuses existing components, STILL draw the cumulative diagram and highlight the new edges/state it adds (e.g. the accept/decline transition, the navigation/track stream). A flow either earns a full diagrammed subsection here or it stays out of §6 — never a half-baked one. For EACH requirement, a subsection: 373 ### 6.x "" 374 - A one-sentence framing of this slice — what the user is trying to do and why this flow is interesting. 375 - Introduce ONLY the new components this slice needs, wired onto what already exists. 376 377 **ARCHITECTURE DECISIONS TABLE** — For EACH new component, write FIVE columns: **Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept** The "What it is" column must define the technology in one plain sentence as if explaining to a smart non-engineer — NO jargon without definition. The "Why THIS choice" column must name the specific reason this problem needs this tool (not generic "it scales" — something precise like "ES because we need ranked full-text search across 100M products in <100ms — no SQL can do that"). The "What we DIDN'T pick" column names 1-2 realistic alternatives and one concrete reason each was rejected (e.g. "Not SQL LIKE: would scan every product row on every search — 100M rows × every query = unusable"; "Not a custom inverted index: months of engineering with no relevance ranking"). Keep this to 2–5 rows per slice. Example format: 378 | Search index | Elasticsearch — a search engine that pre-builds a map of words → products, so a query returns matches in ms | Need ranked full-text search across 100M products in under 100ms | Not SQL LIKE (scans every row, no ranking); not a hand-rolled index (months of work) | Lags behind catalog DB by a few seconds (fine for display) | 379 THEN: a 🎙️ NARRATION block (not the table — this is what the candidate says OUT LOUD) that speaks each choice conversationally: define the technology briefly, say WHY you picked it, say what you considered instead and discarded, and state the trade-off you accepted. One short paragraph per component. The candidate reads this and should be able to say it naturally without memorizing jargon. 380 381 **GLOSSES BLOCK** — After the table, write a dedicated "**🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**" block. For EVERY technical term used in the table or this section (a service name, a pattern name, a protocol), write it as a standalone bolded entry with a plain-English definition + a one-line "why it matters here" sentence. Format: 382 > **Elasticsearch** — [what it is in one simple sentence]. Here we use it because [one line]. 383 > **CDC (Change Data Capture)** — [what it is]. Here we use it because [one line]. 384 > **Kafka** — [what it is]. Here we use it because [one line]. 385 Each entry must be self-contained — the candidate can read just that entry and understand the term. Never bundle multiple terms into one paragraph; each gets its own line. 386 387 **DIAGRAM** — Include a diagram for this slice ONLY if it adds clarity that text alone cannot provide (a new component, a non-obvious data path, a branching flow). If this slice only adds one edge to existing components already fully drawn in a previous subsection, SKIP the diagram and write "↳ builds on §6.x diagram — no new components" instead. When you DO include one: 388 - Use ` ```mermaid flowchart TD ` (top-down) by DEFAULT — this keeps the diagram narrow enough to fit a 13-inch MacBook Air screen without horizontal scrolling. Switch to `flowchart LR` ONLY for a dead-simple 3-4 node linear sequence (A→B→C, no branching) where vertical space would be wasted. 389 - Use `subgraph` blocks to separate conceptually distinct paths (e.g. subgraph "Read Path" vs subgraph "Write / Sync Path"), with a readable title label on each subgraph. 390 - Keep FOCUSED — only the components and edges this flow touches (~8-12 nodes total). 391 - Color palette strictly: clients (blue #4A90D9 fill), our services (green #2E7D4F fill), async/queues (amber #B7791F fill), datastores as database[] cylinders (grey #4A5568 fill), external (purple #6B46C1 fill). ALL node fills must be dark enough to contrast with white text. 392 - EVERY edge must have a label — not just a number, but a short description: `-->|"1: GET /search"|` not just `-->|1|`. Include WHAT is being sent or what the operation is. 393 - Number the request arrows sequentially 1:1 with the prose narration below. 394 - Add a one-line caption above the diagram (bold) naming the flow. 395 - Directly below the diagram, a one-line `↳ reuses existing: (from §6.x)` note. 396 397 **NUMBERED STEP NARRATION** — Walk through each numbered arrow in the diagram. For EACH step write: 398 1. What happens (the action) — in a complete sentence. 399 2. WHY this component handles it (not just "it goes there" but "because this service owns X"). 400 3. What STATE changes (what is different in the system after this step vs before). 401 4. What could go wrong here and how the system handles it (one line — the "what if" every interviewer asks about). 402 This is the richest part of each slice. The candidate should be able to narrate every arrow on the diagram confidently. 403 404 **💬 Say-while-drawing line** — one sentence that captures the KEY insight of this slice, phrased to say out loud while pointing at the diagram. Should name the core tension and the design decision that resolves it. 405 406 **🎙️ Script** — 3–5 connected sentences narrating this slice end-to-end in plain English. Should sound like someone explaining to a colleague, not reading a textbook. Must: define any jargon the first time it appears, state the one architectural decision that makes this slice work, name the trade-off and why it's acceptable. 407 408 End with the ONE full diagram captioned "Final (high-level)" — it MUST be the exact RECONCILED UNION of all the slices: EVERY component that appeared in ANY slice (6.1, 6.2, …) is present here with its same name and color (NOTHING dropped — never lose a cache, DB, or queue that a slice introduced), and it introduces NOTHING that wasn't built in a slice. Use subgraphs in the final diagram to group the "Read Path" components vs "Write / Transaction Path" vs "Async / Events" path so the visual tells the story at a glance. Before finalizing, mentally diff it against the slices and confirm nothing is missing. Annotate load-bearing schema columns inline next to the relevant datastore, then a 🎙️ Script narrating the full flow (2–3 sentences per path, not one blob) and a 🤝 Checkpoint handing the deep-dive choice to the interviewer. 409 410 ## 7. Data Model & Storage 411 **PART A — ENTITY TABLE:** For each entity, one row with: Entity | Key fields (name + type, annotate the PK/shard key) | Chosen store | Shard/partition key | Consistency guarantee Keep field lists tight — only the columns that matter for access patterns or the deep dives. 412 413 **PART B — STORAGE DECISION CARDS:** After the table, for EACH distinct storage choice (each different database/store used), write a standalone "decision card" block. Each card has FIVE parts: 414 415 **🗄️ [Store name] — used for: [entities]** 416 1. **What it is (plain words):** one sentence defining this store as if explaining to a non-engineer. (e.g. "PostgreSQL is a relational database — it stores data in rows and columns with strict rules, and it guarantees that a multi-step operation either fully succeeds or fully rolls back.") 417 2. **Why we picked it — the access pattern that forces this choice:** Be specific — name the EXACT reason this problem needs THIS store. Not "it scales" but "we need an atomic 'decrement-and-check' operation that prevents overselling, and only a relational DB with row-level locking can guarantee that two buyers racing for the last unit can't both win." 418 3. **What we considered instead and why we rejected each:** List 2-3 realistic alternatives with a one-line rejection reason each. Make the rejections concrete — not "it doesn't scale" but "DynamoDB would work for the cart but NOT for inventory — it can't do the conditional atomic decrement we need to prevent overselling without a lot of custom application-side logic." E.g.: 419 - **MongoDB** — flexible schema is nice, but we need ACID multi-row transactions for the order+inventory+payment saga; Mongo's transactions are slower and less battle-tested here. 420 - **DynamoDB** — great for carts (KV by user) but no native conditional decrement strong enough for inventory; also vendor lock-in. 421 - **MySQL** — identical to Postgres for this use case; Postgres has better JSON support and is the standard choice in modern stacks. 422 4. **Trade-off we accept:** one line on what we're giving up (e.g. "operational complexity of managing shards; query joins across shards are impossible so we denormalize"). 423 5. **🗣️ How to say it out loud:** 2-3 natural sentences the candidate can say verbatim — casual, not textbook. (e.g. "For orders and inventory I'm using Postgres, because this is money and I can't afford a half-committed transaction. I looked at Dynamo — it's great for the cart — but it can't do the atomic stock decrement I need without extra complexity. The trade-off is I have to shard manually, but that's fine because I shard by order_id and each shard is independent.") 424 425 **PART C — Per-operation consistency summary:** one compact table: Operation | Store | Consistency level | Why that level is right here Cover: the writes that MUST be strong (money, inventory), the reads that can be eventual (display, search), and the read-your-own-writes edge case. 426 427 **PART D — DATA LIFECYCLE:** For the LARGEST / fastest-growing table (the one from rigor invariant 3 — usually events/analytics/logs), state the retention window and tie it back to the §3 monthly storage $ figure ("at ~50TB/month, 90-day retention caps us at ~150TB — without it we grow unbounded"). Name the reclamation mechanism: time-partitioned tables with a PARTITION DROP (O(1), instant) rather than row-by-row DELETE (which churns the index and never reclaims space cleanly), plus where cold data goes (S3/Glacier archival tier) if it must be kept. One line is enough; the point is showing the big table has an answer to "then what?". 428 429 **🎙️ Script:** 4-6 sentences walking the whole storage tier conversationally — mention the dominant choice (usually Postgres for the core), the KV store, the cache, and the event log. Must name one "I considered X but rejected it because Y" to signal senior-level thinking. 430 431 ## 8. Deep Dives — Bad → Good → Great (the senior signal) 432 This is where the interview is won. Start with a one-line 🆘 "If you get stuck" recovery pointer. Pick the 3–5 hardest problems (driven by the non-functional requirements and edge cases). Pose EACH as a question header: **"### How do we [problem]?"** 433 434 For EACH deep dive, escalate through tiers. Each tier has a FIXED structure — follow it exactly: 435 436 **#### [Bad / Good / Great]: ** 437 438 *(For Good and Great tiers only)* **↩️ What the previous tier got wrong:** Open with one sentence saying exactly what broke in the tier before and WHY this new approach fixes that specific thing. This makes the progression feel like logical problem-solving, not a random list of techniques. E.g. for Good after Bad: "Bad held the lock across the payment call — so I split it: reserve first in a millisecond, pay second, outside any lock." Never skip this line in Good or Great. 439 440 **🗣️ What this is in plain words:** One sentence explaining the approach as if to a smart non-engineer who has never heard of it. No jargon without definition. This line comes FIRST, before any technical description, so the candidate understands what they're about to describe. 441 442 **Approach:** Explain what you actually do in this tier — the mechanism. Be specific about the steps. Every technical term used here must be defined inline the FIRST time it appears in this deep dive (not just in the glosses — right here, parenthetically: e.g. "a row lock (a database mechanism that prevents any other transaction from reading or writing that row until you release it)"). 443 444 **Why this seemed reasonable / Why people try this:** One line — the intuition behind this approach. The candidate should be able to say "I tried X first because it felt natural — it's what most people reach for." 445 446 **⚠️ What breaks (Challenges):** Be concrete — not "it doesn't scale" but "at 500K buyers racing for one item during a flash sale, the lock is held across a payment call that takes 300ms, so 500K threads are now waiting — the database connection pool (the fixed set of open connections the app keeps — typically 50-200) exhausts in seconds, and the whole site stops responding." Name the numbers. Name the failure mode. Name why this matters for THIS system. 447 448 **🔁 What forces the upgrade to the next tier:** one sentence — what exact scenario makes this tier break that the next tier fixes. (Omit on the final Great tier — replace with the failure matrix or trade-offs instead.) 449 450 Tiers are OPTIONAL — skip Bad if the answer is obvious; don't manufacture tiers. For EACH MEANINGFUL tier (usually Good and Great), include a titled mermaid diagram showing ONLY the mechanism for that tier — not the whole system. For the Great tier also include: 451 452 **🔢 Decision-forcing math (if applicable):** Show a quick calculation that proves why this tier matters — e.g. "500K buyers × 300ms lock hold ÷ 200 connection pool = exhaustion in under 1 second." Keep it rounded and talkable. 453 454 **✅ Failure matrix (for correctness-critical deep dives):** A compact table of every failure scenario and what happens — e.g. "Payment succeeds but commit crashes → reconcile by idempotency key → complete commit, no double-charge." Cover: success, failure, timeout, retry, partial failure, crash-after-payment. 455 456 Each deep dive ends with: 457 - **🎙️ Script:** 3-5 connected sentences saying the Great solution out loud, in plain conversational English. Define every term the first time. Sound like an engineer explaining to a colleague, not reading slides. 458 - **🧠 "If they ask…":** the single most obvious pushback and a 2-sentence answer. 459 460 Cover where relevant: the binding bottleneck + its relief + the new ceiling after the fix; hot-key / celebrity-item problems; idempotency and duplicate suppression; every global coordinator (anything that is a single point of serialization); consistency stance (strong vs eventual) and read-your-own-writes; what happens when each piece fails. 461 462 ## 9. Reliability, Failure Modes & Cost 463 Structure this as FOUR named sub-sections, each with plain-English definitions for every term: 464 465 **9A — Availability targets (what the "nines" mean):** 466 For each path (read path, money/write path), state the availability target as "X nines" AND what that means in human terms: "four nines = 52 minutes of downtime per year total; three nines = 8.7 hours." Then state the mechanisms that achieve it (CDN, replicas, multi-AZ failover). Make the mechanism readable: define "multi-AZ" (multiple data centres in the same region so if one building loses power the other takes over automatically) the first time it appears. 467 468 **9B — Per-dependency failure table:** 469 For EACH major component, one row: Component | What breaks if it goes down | How the system degrades gracefully | Recovery action. The "graceful degradation" column must describe what the USER SEES — not just "falls back to replica" but "users can still browse categories but search results might be slightly stale." Define every fallback mechanism inline (e.g. "serve from Redis cache (the in-memory store) until the search cluster recovers"). 470 471 **9C — RPO / RTO per data class (plain definitions FIRST):** 472 Define RPO and RTO in plain words before using them: "RPO (Recovery Point Objective) = how much data we're OK losing if a crash happens right now. RPO≈0 means we lose nothing — every write is synchronously replicated before we acknowledge it. RTO (Recovery Time Objective) = how long before the service is working again after a crash." Then give a table: Data class | RPO | RTO | Mechanism. Cover: orders/payments (RPO≈0), catalog (looser — can rebuild from source), search index (rebuildable, minutes of lag OK). 473 474 **9D — Cost breakdown:** 475 Name the top 3 cost drivers and WHY each is expensive in plain terms (e.g. "CDN egress — every byte of image data served to a user's browser is charged by the CDN; at millions of page views a day this is the biggest line item, not the database"). Give a rough monthly order-of-magnitude. Name the one optimization that would cut cost the most. 476 477 **🎙️ Script:** 3-4 sentences on the failure story — what the user EXPERIENCES when each major thing fails, not just what the system does internally. 478 479 ## 10. Trade-off Ledger 480 For EACH of the 2–4 most significant design decisions, write a DECISION CARD with five parts: 481 482 **🔀 Decision: [what you chose] vs [what you didn't]** 483 1. **What we chose and why:** one line — the core reason. 484 2. **What we gave up:** one concrete thing that's harder or worse because of this choice. 485 3. **🗣️ Plain words:** one sentence a non-engineer can understand — "think of it like choosing a fast highway that's slightly less reliable over a slower but guaranteed road." 486 4. **When this reverses:** the exact condition that would make you flip this decision — be specific ("if flash sales are removed from scope, we drop the Redis counter and go back to a single Postgres row — simpler, no reconciliation needed"; "if we owned the payment processor in-house, we could do a real two-phase commit instead of a saga"). 487 5. **🗣️ How to say it:** one sentence the candidate can say out loud — natural, not textbook. 488 489 Tie each decision back to the non-functional requirements and any "⚠️ Gaps" from §1. **🎙️ Script:** 3-4 sentences walking the ledger — the decisions, the trade-offs, and what would make you change your mind. 490 491 ## 11. Likely Interviewer Questions & Answers 492 Cover ALL of these question domains — generate 2-3 questions per domain, 12-18 total: (1) Core algorithm / the hardest part of THIS specific problem (e.g. "two buyers race for the last unit — who wins?"), (2) Failure scenarios ("what if the payment processor goes down?", "what if Redis crashes?"), (3) Scale / hot-spot ("how do you handle a flash sale?"), (4) Consistency / correctness ("can the same order be placed twice?", "can you oversell?"), (5) Security ("how do you stop price tampering?", "what if someone forges a user id?"), (6) Cost ("what's the most expensive part?", "how would you cut costs?"), (7) Extensibility ("how would you add feature X?", "how would you support Y?"). Do NOT generate only checkout/core questions — a real interview covers all domains. 493 494 For EACH question, write FOUR parts: 495 496 **❓ [The question exactly as an interviewer would say it]** 497 498 **The mechanism (what actually happens):** 2-3 sentences. Every term used that hasn't been defined earlier must be defined inline here. 499 500 **🗣️ In plain words:** 1-2 sentences — same answer, zero jargon. This is what the candidate says if they blank on the technical version: "In simple terms, [plain version]." 501 502 **💬 One-liner to say out loud:** the sharpest single sentence — confident, not textbook. 503 504 Answers must be REAL and SPECIFIC — not "we use caching" but "the product page is in Redis with a 60-second TTL; during a flash sale the cache absorbs ~8K requests/s so Postgres never sees the spike." No one-liners as the main answer. Mechanism + plain words + one-liner every time. 505 506 End with a **🎙️ "60-second verbal summary"** — follow this template exactly, filling in the specifics for this problem: "[1 sentence: what the system is in plain words]. The design splits into two halves. [2-3 sentences: the READ HALF — what it is, what technologies, why it works]. [2-3 sentences: the WRITE / TRANSACTION HALF — what it is, the hardest problem in it, and the one mechanism that solves it]. [1 sentence: how the two halves connect — usually an event stream or async pipeline]. [1 sentence: the single design principle that every hard decision traces back to]." Write this as flowing connected sentences — no bullet points, no section headers. It should sound like someone giving a confident wrap-up to a colleague, not reading notes. 507 508

content.text: I've read both prompt files. Now let me set up the workspace directory and write the capacity.py file to compute the numbers.

input.description: Create workspace dir and verify venv python exists

input.content:
"""Capacity model for a URL Shortener (TinyURL / Bitly). Read-heavy.
All numbers rounded for talkability in the writeup; this file shows the derivation."""

def human(n):
    if n >= 1e12: return f"{n/1e12:.1f}T"
    if n >= 1e9:  return f"{n/1e9:.1f}B"
    if n >= 1e6:  return f"{n/1e6:.1f}M"
    if n >= 1e3:  return f"{n/1e3:.1f}K"
    return f"{n:.0f}"

SEC_PER_DAY = 86_400

# ---- Assumptions (locked in scope lock) ----
NEW_URLS_PER_DAY = 100_000_000        # 100M new short links / day (Bitly-class, generous)
READ_WRITE_RATIO = 100                # 100:1 reads:writes (redirect-heavy)
PEAK_FACTOR = 5                       # burst factor over daily-average (diurnal + viral spikes)
RETENTION_YEARS = 5                   # how long links live before archival
CACHE_HIT_RATIO = 0.95               # hot links served from cache (Zipfian access)

# ---- Writes (create short link) ----
writes_per_day = NEW_URLS_PER_DAY
write_qps_avg = writes_per_day / SEC_PER_DAY
write_qps_peak = write_qps_avg * PEAK_FACTOR

# ---- Reads (redirects) ----
reads_per_day = writes_per_day * READ_WRITE_RATIO
read_qps_avg = reads_per_day / SEC_PER_DAY
read_qps_peak = read_qps_avg * PEAK_FACTOR

# ---- Cache miss QPS (what actually hits the DB) ----
miss_qps_peak = read_qps_peak * (1 - CACHE_HIT_RATIO)
miss_qps_cold = read_qps_peak  # 0% hit, cold cache worst case

# ---- Redis ceiling check ----
REDIS_OPS_PER_NODE = 150_000          # ~150K ops/s per Redis node (single-threaded)
redis_nodes_needed = read_qps_peak / REDIS_OPS_PER_NODE

# ---- DB read replica check (miss path) ----
DB_READ_QPS_PER_REPLICA = 10_000      # ~10K point-reads/s per Postgres read replica (PK lookup)
replicas_needed = miss_qps_peak / DB_READ_QPS_PER_REPLICA

# ---- Storage: mapping table footprint ----
total_links_5yr = NEW_URLS_PER_DAY * 365 * RETENTION_YEARS
# per row: short_code 7B + long_url ~200B avg + owner_id 8B + created_at 8B
#          + expires_at 8B + flags 4B + row/page overhead ~50B
BYTES_PER_ROW = 7 + 200 + 8 + 8 + 8 + 4 + 50    # ~285B logical
# index on short_code (PK) + secondary index on owner_id: ~2x of key-sized entries
INDEX_MULTIPLIER = 1.6
REPLICATION_FACTOR = 3
COMPACTION_HEADROOM = 1.5             # WAL + bloat + free-space headroom
raw_table_bytes = total_links_5yr * BYTES_PER_ROW
full_footprint = raw_table_bytes * INDEX_MULTIPLIER * REPLICATION_FACTOR * COMPACTION_HEADROOM

# ---- Storage: click-events (analytics) table — the LARGEST table ----
# every redirect logs an event; raw events kept 90 days then rolled to aggregates+S3
EVENT_BYTES = 100                     # short_code 7B + ts 8B + geo/ua/referrer hashed ~75B + overhead
events_per_day = reads_per_day
RAW_EVENT_RETENTION_DAYS = 90
raw_events_bytes = events_per_day * RAW_EVENT_RETENTION_DAYS * EVENT_BYTES * REPLICATION_FACTOR

# ---- Cost (very rough monthly order-of-magnitude) ----
# 1) Cache fleet
COST_PER_REDIS_NODE_MO = 1_500        # ~cache.r6g.xlarge-ish reserved, blended
redis_fleet_cost = max(3, round(redis_nodes_needed) + 1) * COST_PER_REDIS_NODE_MO
# 2) DB fleet (primary + replicas)
COST_PER_DB_NODE_MO = 2_500
db_nodes = 1 + max(2, round(replicas_needed))   # primary + replicas, min 2 replicas
db_fleet_cost = db_nodes * COST_PER_DB_NODE_MO
# 3) Analytics/event storage + OLAP + object store
analytics_cost_mo = 25_000           # Kafka + stream processor + columnar warehouse + S3, blended
# 4) Egress/LB for redirects (redirects are tiny 301/302 headers, cheap)
COST_PER_MILLION_REQ = 0.50          # LB + bandwidth for header-only redirects, blended
monthly_reqs = reads_per_day * 30
egress_cost_mo = (monthly_reqs / 1e6) * COST_PER_MILLION_REQ
total_cost_mo = redis_fleet_cost + db_fleet_cost + analytics_cost_mo + egress_cost_mo

# ---- Unit economics ----
cost_per_million_reads = total_cost_mo / (monthly_reqs / 1e6)

# ---- Key space check ----
BASE62 = 62
for L in range(5, 9):
    print(f"  base62^{L} = {human(BASE62**L)} codes")

print("="*64)
print("WRITES")
print(f"  writes/day            = {human(writes_per_day)}")
print(f"  write QPS avg         = {write_qps_avg:,.0f}  -> ~{human(write_qps_avg)}/s")
print(f"  write QPS peak (x{PEAK_FACTOR}) = {write_qps_peak:,.0f}  -> ~{human(write_qps_peak)}/s")
print("READS (redirects)")
print(f"  read:write ratio      = {READ_WRITE_RATIO}:1")
print(f"  reads/day             = {human(reads_per_day)}")
print(f"  read QPS avg          = {read_qps_avg:,.0f}  -> ~{human(read_qps_avg)}/s")
print(f"  read QPS peak (x{PEAK_FACTOR})  = {read_qps_peak:,.0f}  -> ~{human(read_qps_peak)}/s")

input.content:
# Design a URL Shortener (TinyURL / Bitly)

> One line frame: we take a long URL, hand back a short code, and on every click we redirect that code back to the original — at a roughly 100:1 read-to-write ratio, so this is overwhelmingly a **read hot-path** problem. The whole game is making the redirect fast, cheap, and never-wrong, while generating short codes that *never collide*.

---

## 1. Scope, Requirements & Assumptions

> 🎙️ "Here's how I'll structure this: I'll lock scope with a couple of quick assumptions, state the requirements and rough scale, sketch the API and data model, draw the high-level design one requirement at a time, then go deep on the two or three hardest parts — short-code generation, the read hot-path, and the analytics pipeline — checking in with you as I go. Since this is an Amazon-style bar, I'll keep cost and operations first-class and work backwards from the customer. Sound good?"

> 💬 "The core tension is simple to state and hard to nail: this thing is read-dominated by ~100:1, so I'll spend almost my entire budget making the redirect cheap, and I'll obsess over one correctness invariant — short codes must be unique *by construction*, never by luck."

### 🔒 Scope lock (the architecture-forking assumptions — veto any of these)

I'll state the few questions whose answers *fork the whole design* as assumptions you can stop me on:

1. **Reads dominate ~100:1 and we're a single-region write tier to start.** Almost all traffic is redirects; creates are rare. *— stop me if writes are actually heavy or you want active-active multi-region writes from day one.*
2. **Short-code generation must be collision-free by construction, not by retry.** I'll assume we cannot tolerate "generate-then-check-then-maybe-retry" loops at write scale. *— stop me if a tiny collision-retry rate is acceptable.*
3. **Custom aliases are in scope** (e.g. `/my-brand`), so the code namespace is shared between auto-generated and user-chosen codes. *— stop me if custom aliases are out; that removes a whole collision class.*
4. **Analytics is "good enough" eventual, not exact.** Click counts can lag a few seconds and we tolerate at-least-once counting with dedup. *— stop me if you need exact, real-time, billable click counts.*

> 🤝 **In/out-of-scope checkpoint:** "In scope: shorten, redirect, custom alias, async click analytics. Out: user accounts/billing UI, link editing/preview pages, malware/phishing scanning, A-B link rotation. Add or cut before I commit?"

### Functional requirements (derived from the locked assumptions — "above the line")

Since we assumed a shared, collision-free namespace with custom aliases and heavy reads, the core flows are:

1. **Shorten a long URL → short code.** `POST` a long URL, get back `https://sho.rt/aZ3kP9`.
   - 🗣️ **Plain words:** someone pastes a giant URL and we hand them a tiny one that points to the same place.
   - ⚡ **Why it's hard:** the short code must be globally unique *without* a slow check-then-retry on every write, and it must coexist with user-chosen custom aliases in the same namespace.
2. **Redirect short code → long URL.** `GET /aZ3kP9` returns an HTTP redirect to the original.
   - 🗣️ **Plain words:** when anyone clicks the short link, their browser instantly lands on the real page.
   - ⚡ **Why it's hard:** this is ~580K requests/s at peak; it must be single-digit-millisecond and survive a cache flush — and the 301-vs-302 choice silently decides whether analytics even works.
3. **Custom alias.** Let a user request a specific code (`/spring-sale`) if it's free.
   - 🗣️ **Plain words:** a brand can pick its own memorable short link instead of random characters.
   - ⚡ **Why it's hard:** custom aliases and auto-generated codes share one namespace, so we need an atomic "claim if free" that can't let two creators win the same alias.
4. **Async click analytics.** Count clicks per code (and rough geo/referrer) without slowing the redirect.
   - 🗣️ **Plain words:** the link owner can see how many people clicked and roughly from where.
   - ⚡ **Why it's hard:** we cannot do a synchronous DB write on the 580K/s hot path — counting has to ride a fire-and-forget pipeline that's lossy-tolerant but de-duplicated.

**Below the line (out of scope):** user auth/billing, link editing & expiry-management UI, branded-domain onboarding, phishing/malware scanning, QR codes, link preview/unfurl, A-B rotation.

### Non-Functional requirements (PROVISIONAL targets, tagged to the assumption they depend on)

1. **Redirect availability ≥ 99.99% ("four nines").** *(depends on assumption 1: read-dominated)*
   - 🗣️ **Plain words:** the redirect path is down less than ~52 minutes per *year*, total.
   - 💥 **What breaks without it:** every existing short link everywhere — in emails, tweets, printed QR codes — silently 404s; the redirect is the product, so an outage is total.
2. **Redirect p99 ≤ ~50ms server-side on a cache hit (sub-10ms typical).** *(depends on assumption 1)*
   - 🗣️ **Plain words:** 99 out of 100 clicks resolve in well under a tenth of a second.
   - 💥 **What breaks without it:** slow redirects feel like broken links; users abandon and link owners stop using us.
3. **Code uniqueness = strong/exact (zero collisions).** *(depends on assumption 2 + 3)*
   - 🗣️ **Plain words:** no two long URLs ever get the same short code, and no custom alias ever overwrites someone else's.
   - 💥 **What breaks without it:** a collision sends a user to the *wrong* site — a correctness and trust catastrophe (think: a banking link resolving to a phishing page).
4. **Analytics: eventual, at-least-once + dedup, lag ≤ a few seconds.** *(depends on assumption 4)*
   - 🗣️ **Plain words:** click counts are allowed to be a few seconds stale and we dedup retries so we don't over-count.
   - 💥 **What breaks without it:** either we slow the hot path (synchronous counting) or we lose/double-count clicks and owners stop trusting the dashboard.

> ⚠️ **Gaps I'd flag out loud:** if click analytics ever becomes *billable* (pay-per-click), eventual at-least-once isn't good enough and I'd need an exactly-once, reconciled counting tier — I'll revisit this in the ledger.

> 🎙️ **Script:** "So the product is four flows: shorten, redirect, custom alias, and async analytics. The redirect is the whole ballgame — it's ~100x the write traffic, so I want it on a cache at single-digit milliseconds and four-nines available. The one correctness rule I will not bend is that short codes are unique by construction — a collision means sending someone to the wrong site, which is unacceptable. Analytics I'll treat as eventual and lossy-tolerant so it never touches the hot path. Everything else — accounts, billing, malware scanning — I'm putting below the line unless you want it."

---

## 2. The Forks Behind My Assumptions (what changes if I'm wrong)

These are the residual forks from the scope lock — the three places my design is most sensitive. I'm not re-asking scale (I've committed to ~100:1 reads and single-region writes); I'm exposing where the architecture would *bend*.

**Fork A — Code generation strategy (I locked "collision-free by construction").**
- ↔️ **What changes if I'm wrong:**
  - If a small collision-retry rate is fine → I can use a **random base62 / hash-prefix** generator with a DB uniqueness check and retry-on-collision, no global coordinator at all.
  - If it truly must be zero-collision and zero-retry → I need a **counter + base62** scheme behind a global ID allocator (a key-generation service handing out pre-minted ranges), which I'll defend as the primary.
- 🗣️ **How I'd say it:** "I'm assuming we want codes that are unique without a check-and-retry loop, so I'll use a counter. If you're OK with a one-in-a-billion retry on write, random codes are simpler — tell me and I'll switch."

**Fork B — 301 vs 302 redirect (I locked "analytics must work, so we lean 302").**
- ↔️ **What changes if I'm wrong:**
  - If SEO/permanence and offloading traffic from our servers matter more than per-click analytics → use **301 (permanent)**: browsers and intermediaries cache it, so we serve fewer redirects, but we *stop seeing most clicks*.
  - If analytics and the ability to change the destination matter more → use **302 (temporary / found)**: every click comes back to us, so we count every click and can repoint a link, at the cost of more redirect traffic.
- 🗣️ **How I'd say it:** "I'll default to 302 so every click hits us and analytics is real — if you'd rather offload traffic and don't care about click data, we flip to 301 and accept that browsers stop telling us about clicks."

**Fork C — Analytics accuracy (I locked "eventual, at-least-once + dedup").**
- ↔️ **What changes if I'm wrong:**
  - If counts are *display-only* → an async fire-and-forget pipeline with approximate aggregation is plenty (cheapest).
  - If counts become *billable/exact* → I need durable ingest (a log we can replay), idempotent consumers keyed on an event id, and periodic reconciliation — a meaningfully heavier pipeline.
- 🗣️ **How I'd say it:** "If clicks are just a vanity dashboard, my async pipeline is fine; if you're charging customers per click, I'll harden it to exactly-once with reconciliation."

> 🤝 **Checkpoint:** "Those are the three forks I care about — generation strategy, 301-vs-302, and analytics accuracy. Want to lock any of them differently before I size capacity?"

---

## 3. Scale & Capacity (talkable numbers)

I computed these with a small `capacity.py` rather than hand-waving. **QPS** = queries per second (requests hitting us each second). **TTL** = time-to-live, how long a cached value lives before it expires. I derive a **peak** from the daily average with a stated **burst factor** of 5x (diurnal swing + viral link spikes), and I size the read tier from **miss QPS** (the fraction of reads that escape the cache and hit the DB), not from cache size.

| Metric | Derivation (step-by-step) | Rounded | Talkable phrase | Decision it drives |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| New links/day | assume 100M creates/day (Bitly-class) | 100M/day | "a hundred million new links a day" | sets write tier + keyspace |
| Write QPS (peak) | 100M ÷ 86,400 ≈ 1.2K/s avg; ×5 burst | ~6K/s | "about six thousand writes a second at peak" | a single Postgres primary handles this — writes are *not* the problem |
| Read QPS (peak) | 100M × 100 = 10B reads/day; ÷86,400 ≈ 116K/s avg; ×5 burst | **~580K/s** | "about half a million redirects a second at peak" | **THE decision-forcing number** |
| Cache miss QPS (peak) | 580K/s × (1 − 0.95 hit) | ~29K/s | "about thirty thousand misses a second" | sizes DB read replicas, not cache |
| Cold-cache QPS (0% hit) | full 580K/s hits DB | ~580K/s | "if the cache is cold, half a million land on the DB" | forces gradual cache warm-up + request coalescing |
| Redis nodes | 580K/s ÷ ~150K ops/s per node | ~4 (round up + headroom) | "four-ish cache nodes" | shard/replicate the cache |
| DB read replicas | 29K miss/s ÷ ~10K reads/s per replica | ~3 | "about three read replicas" | replica fan-out for misses |
| Keyspace (base62) | 62^7 ≈ 3.5T; 62^6 ≈ 57B | **7 chars** | "seven characters gives three-and-a-half trillion codes" | code length = 7 |
| Mapping table (5yr) | 182B rows × ~285B × idx 1.6 × repl 3 × headroom 1.5 | ~375TB | "a few hundred terabytes" | shard the mapping store |
| Click-events table (90d raw) | 10B/day × 90d × 100B × repl 3 | **~270TB** | "the biggest table by far" | drives the data-lifecycle / partition-drop story |
| Cost (rough) | redis ~$8K + db ~$10K + analytics ~$25K + egress/LB ~$150K | **~$190K/mo** | "around two hundred K a month" | egress/LB dominates, not the DB |
| Unit economics | $190K ÷ 300B reqs/mo | **~$0.64 / 1M redirects** | "under a dollar per million clicks" | the number I'd put on a one-pager |

**Keyspace math (plain text):** base62 = digits + lower + upper = 62 symbols. 62^6 ≈ 57 billion (too tight against 182B links over 5 years), 62^7 ≈ 3.5 trillion (comfortable headroom even at our 182B). **So 7 characters.**

**The ONE decision-forcing number + flip threshold:** ~**580K reads/s at peak**. A single Redis node tops out around **150K ops/s** (it's effectively single-threaded). 580K ÷ 150K ≈ 4, so the cache *must* be sharded/replicated — a single cache node is impossible. **Flip threshold:** if peak reads ever fell below ~150K/s (e.g. a private/internal shortener), I'd collapse to one cache node and skip sharding entirely.

> 🎙️ **Script:** "The number that drives everything is about half a million redirects a second at peak — that's a hundred-to-one over writes, which are only about six thousand a second. A single cache node maxes out near a hundred-fifty thousand ops a second, so I have to spread reads across a handful of cache nodes; writes I'll barely think about. Storage is a few hundred terabytes for the mapping and the bigger worry is the click-events table at a couple hundred terabytes — I'll partition-drop that. And interestingly the biggest *cost* line isn't the database, it's the load-balancer and bandwidth for serving billions of tiny redirects — about two hundred K a month total, under a dollar per million clicks."

---

## 4. Core Entities

This is a first-draft scaffold; full fields land in §7 (Data Model).

- **ShortLink** — the mapping. Owns: `short_code → long_url`, owner, created/expires timestamps, and whether it's a custom alias. It's the heart of the system.
  - 🗣️ **Analogy:** like a single line in a phone book — a short name on the left, the real number on the right.
  - ⚠️ **Probe:** "How do you guarantee the short_code is unique?" → it's the primary key; auto-generated codes come from a counter (unique by construction), custom aliases use an atomic insert-if-absent so a duplicate just fails.
- **Counter / ID range** — the global allocator state behind auto-generated codes (a monotonically increasing number we base62-encode).
  - 🗣️ **Analogy:** like a deli ticket dispenser — every customer gets the next number, nobody shares one.
  - ⚠️ **Probe:** "Isn't that a single bottleneck?" → no, the allocator hands out *ranges* (e.g. 1,000 IDs) to each app server, so servers mint codes locally and only touch the allocator once per range.
- **ClickEvent** — one record per redirect for analytics: code, timestamp, coarse geo/referrer/user-agent.
  - 🗣️ **Analogy:** like a turnstile counter — every time someone walks through (clicks), it ticks.
  - ⚠️ **Probe:** "Won't writing this slow every redirect?" → it never blocks the redirect; we fire the event onto a queue and a separate worker aggregates it.
- **Owner / Account** *(thin, mostly out of scope)* — who created the link, for authz and per-owner listing.
  - 🗣️ **Analogy:** like the name on a library card — it just says whose link this is.
  - ⚠️ **Probe:** "Can someone read another user's link analytics?" → object-level authz: we check the caller's id from their token matches the link's `owner_id`.

> 🎙️ **Script:** "There are really four entities. The star is the ShortLink — code to long URL, that's the whole product. Behind it is a Counter that mints unique numbers we base62-encode into codes. ClickEvents are the analytics records, and they ride a queue so they never touch the redirect. There's a thin Owner for authz. I'll flesh out exact fields and keys in the data model, but the surprising one is the Counter — it sounds like a single bottleneck, and a big part of the design is making sure it isn't."

---

## 5. API / Interface

Everything is REST under `/v1`. **VERSIONING:** prefix all paths with `/v1`; additive changes (a new optional field) don't bump the version, breaking changes get `/v2`. **PAGINATION:** any list endpoint (e.g. a user's links, or analytics rows) uses a **cursor/keyset token**, not offset/limit — offset gets slower as it skips more rows and can skip or duplicate rows when data shifts under it. **SECURITY:** the caller is identified from a **JWT** *(a signed token in the request header that proves who the user is — we never trust a client-supplied user id)*; every mutating or owner-scoped endpoint does **object-level authz** ("does this caller own *this* link?"), not just "are they logged in?".

### 5.1 Shorten a URL — `POST /v1/links`
- **Why POST:** it creates new server state (a new mapping), so POST, not GET.
- **Key fields:**
  - `longUrl: string` — the destination; server validates it's a well-formed http(s) URL.
  - `customAlias: string?` — optional; if present we attempt to claim exactly that code.
  - `expiresAt: string?` — optional TTL for the link.
  - `Idempotency-Key: <uuid>` (header) — so a client retry after a network blip doesn't mint two different codes for the same intent.
  - *Absent on purpose:* `shortCode` for the auto case (the **server** mints it — clients can't pick random codes); `ownerId` (taken from the JWT, never the body, so no one can create links as someone else).
```
// Request
POST /v1/links
Idempotency-Key: 7c1f...e9
{ "longUrl": "https://example.com/very/long/path?utm=spring", "customAlias": "spring-sale", "expiresAt": "2026-12-31T00:00:00Z" }

// Response 201
{ "shortCode": "spring-sale", "shortUrl": "https://sho.rt/spring-sale", "longUrl": "https://example.com/very/long/path?utm=spring", "createdAt": "2026-06-21T10:00:00Z" }

// Response 409 (alias taken)
{ "error": "ALIAS_TAKEN", "message": "spring-sale is already in use" }
```
- **Key decision — idempotency + alias claim:** the `Idempotency-Key` lets a retried create return the *same* code instead of a second one; the custom-alias path is an **atomic insert-if-absent** that returns 409 on conflict rather than overwriting.

### 5.2 Redirect — `GET /{shortCode}`
- **Why GET + a redirect (not JSON):** browsers follow HTTP redirects natively; returning JSON would require client code, which defeats "paste a link anywhere."
- **Key fields:** just the path code. No body.
```
// Request
GET /aZ3kP9   Host: sho.rt

// Response 302 (default — keeps analytics)
HTTP/1.1 302 Found
Location: https://example.com/very/long/path?utm=spring
Cache-Control: private, max-age=0
```
- **Key decision — 302 vs 301:** I return **302 Found** *(temporary redirect — the browser comes back to us next time)* by default so every click is observable for analytics and so we can repoint the link later. **301 Moved Permanently** *(browser caches the mapping and skips us)* would cut our traffic and cost but blind us to clicks; it's a per-product flip (Fork B). For a 404 (unknown code) we return a small branded page, not a stack trace.

### 5.3 Custom alias — same `POST /v1/links` with `customAlias`
- Covered by 5.1; the only addition is the **409 ALIAS_TAKEN** contract. Object-level note: a custom alias must also pass a denylist (no impersonating reserved paths like `api`, `admin`, `login`).

### 5.4 Read analytics — `GET /v1/links/{shortCode}/stats`
- **Why GET:** read-only.
- **Key fields:** `from`, `to` (time window), `cursor` (keyset pagination for raw rows).
```
// Request
GET /v1/links/aZ3kP9/stats?from=2026-06-01&to=2026-06-21&cursor=eyJ0cyI6...

// Response 200
{ "shortCode": "aZ3kP9", "totalClicks": 184213, "byCountry": { "US": 90112, "IN": 41200 }, "windowFrom": "2026-06-01", "windowTo": "2026-06-21", "nextCursor": "eyJ0cyI6Li4ufQ" }
```
- **Key decision — object-level authz:** we check `caller.sub == link.ownerId` before returning stats; counts are served from a pre-aggregated store and are *eventually* consistent (a few seconds stale is fine).

> 💬 "Notice the client never sends `ownerId` or, for the auto case, the `shortCode` — the server owns both. That's how we stop someone forging links as another user or grabbing a code they shouldn't."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Four endpoints. POST to create a link — with an idempotency key so a retry doesn't mint two codes, and the owner comes from the token, never the body. GET on the bare code is the redirect; I return a 302 by default so analytics keeps working. Custom alias reuses the create endpoint but can 409 if the alias is taken. And a stats endpoint that's owner-scoped with object-level authz and cursor pagination. Everything's under slash-v-one so I can evolve it without breaking old links."

---

## 6. High-Level Design (built one functional requirement at a time)

Color convention (reused in every diagram): **blue = clients**, **green = our services (hot path)**, **amber = async/queues**, **grey = datastores/caches (cylinders)**, **purple = external**.

### 6.1 "Shorten a long URL → short code"

We're turning a long URL into a 7-char code that's unique *by construction*. The interesting part is the code generator and where its state lives.

**Architecture decisions table**

| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Write Service | Stateless app servers that handle `POST /v1/links` | Simple, horizontally scalable; writes are only ~6K/s so this is easy | Not a single big monolith node (no HA, can't scale a hot path independently) | More moving parts, need a load balancer in front |
| Key Generation Service (KGS) | A tiny service that hands each Write Service a *range* of pre-minted IDs (e.g. 1,000 numbers) from a durable counter | Makes codes unique by construction with almost no per-write coordination | Not random+check-and-retry (extra DB read per write, retries under load); not per-write counter calls (KGS becomes a 6K/s hot spot) | Burned/lost IDs when a server crashes mid-range — fine, the keyspace is 3.5T |
| Base62 encoder | Pure function: integer → 7-char `[0-9a-zA-Z]` string | Compact, URL-safe, deterministic; no DB round-trip | Not hashing the URL (hashes collide and need a check); not UUIDs (too long, ugly) | None meaningful |
| Mapping store (Postgres, sharded) | Relational DB holding `short_code → long_url`, sharded by `short_code` | Need an atomic insert-if-absent for custom aliases and a strong PK uniqueness guarantee | Not a plain KV without conditional writes (can't cleanly enforce alias uniqueness); not one unsharded DB (375TB won't fit) | Cross-shard queries are hard, so we denormalize and key everything by code |

> 🎙️ **Narration:** "The Write Service is just stateless app servers — writes are light so this is the boring part. The clever bit is the KGS: instead of asking a central counter for a number on every write, each server grabs a block of a thousand IDs at once and mints codes locally, so the counter is touched once per thousand creates, not once per create. I base62-encode the number into a 7-character code — base62 just means I have 62 symbols, the digits plus both cases of letters, so seven characters gives me trillions of codes. I considered hashing the URL or using random strings, but both can collide and force a check-and-retry on every write; the counter is unique by construction. The store is Postgres sharded by code because custom aliases need an atomic insert-if-absent that a dumb key-value store can't cleanly give me."

> **🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
> **KGS (Key Generation Service)** — a small service that pre-allocates blocks of unique IDs from a durable counter. Here it lets every app server mint codes without hitting a central counter on each write.
> **base62** — encoding a number using 62 symbols (0-9, a-z, A-Z). Here it turns a big integer into a short, URL-safe code.
> **Insert-if-absent** — a DB write that succeeds only if the key doesn't already exist. Here it makes claiming a custom alias atomic so two creators can't both win.
> **Shard** — splitting one logical table across many DB machines by a key. Here we shard by `short_code` so reads and writes for a code always land on one machine.

**Diagram — create flow**
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Client[Client app]:::client
    Client -->|"1: POST v1 links"| LB[Load balancer]:::svc
    LB -->|"2: route"| WS[Write Service]:::svc
    WS -->|"3: need a code"| KGS[Key Generation Service]:::svc
    KGS -->|"4: grant ID range"| CounterDB[(Counter store)]:::db
    WS -->|"5: base62 encode locally"| WS
    WS -->|"6: insert mapping"| Mapping[(Mapping store sharded)]:::db
    WS -->|"7: return shortUrl"| Client

    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3

↳ reuses existing: none (first slice)

**Numbered step narration**
1. **Client POSTs the long URL** to the load balancer. Why LB: it spreads ~6K writes/s and gives HA. State: none yet. What goes wrong: a network retry — handled by the `Idempotency-Key`.
2. **LB routes to a Write Service** instance. Why: stateless services are interchangeable. State: none.
3. **Write Service asks KGS for capacity** *only if its local range is exhausted*. Why: amortizes the counter. State: server about to receive a new range.
4. **KGS atomically bumps the durable counter** and grants a range (e.g. IDs 5,001,000–5,001,999). Why: one atomic increment per 1,000 creates. State: counter advanced; range is now "owned" by that server. What goes wrong: server crash burns the range — acceptable, 3.5T keyspace.
5. **Write Service base62-encodes the next ID locally.** Why: pure function, no I/O. State: a candidate code exists.
6. **Write Service inserts the mapping** (`code → long_url`) on the shard owned by that code. For a custom alias this is insert-if-absent → 409 on conflict. State: link is durable. What goes wrong: alias collision → 409 returned, no overwrite.
7. **Returns the short URL.** State: client has its link.

> 💬 "The whole trick on writes is: don't call the counter per write — grab a block of a thousand and mint locally. The counter goes from a 6K/s hot spot to a 6-per-second nudge."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "To create a link, a stateless write service grabs a code. It mints codes from a block of IDs the key-generation service handed it — base62-encoding a number — so the only correctness rule, uniqueness, is guaranteed by construction instead of by retrying. It writes the mapping into Postgres sharded by code, and for a custom alias it's an atomic insert-if-absent so two people can't claim the same alias. The trade-off is we sometimes waste a block of IDs when a server dies, but with trillions of codes that's free."

### 6.2 "Redirect short code → long URL"

This is the hot path: ~580K/s. The whole design is "answer from cache, almost never touch the DB." It builds directly on 6.1's mapping store.

**Architecture decisions table**

| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| CDN / edge | Geographically distributed cache of redirect responses near users | Terminates most traffic near the user, cuts latency and origin load | Not origin-only (every click crosses the internet to us) | Edge caching a 302 means short max-age, so we still see most clicks |
| Redirect Service | Stateless app servers that resolve a code to a URL and emit a 302 | Tiny, fast, horizontally scalable | Not serving directly from the DB (DB can't do 580K/s point reads) | Another tier to operate |
| Redis cache (sharded) | In-memory store of `code → long_url`, sharded across nodes | 580K/s ÷ 150K/node ⇒ must shard; in-memory = sub-ms reads | Not a local in-process cache only (can't share across many servers, cold on deploy); not Memcached (Redis gives us richer ops + persistence options) | Cache and DB can briefly disagree; we use TTL + write-through |
| Mapping store (Postgres replicas) | Same store from 6.1, read replicas for cache misses | Misses are only ~29K/s ⇒ ~3 replicas absorb them | Not hitting the primary for reads (would compete with writes) | Replica lag (ms) — fine, mappings are immutable after create |

> 🎙️ **Narration:** "On a click, I want to never touch the database. The request hits a CDN — a network of caches near the user — then a stateless redirect service, then Redis, an in-memory cache. Redis is in-memory so reads are sub-millisecond, and I shard it because one node tops out around a hundred-fifty thousand ops a second and I'm at half a million. Only the ~5% of reads that miss go to a Postgres read replica — that's about thirty thousand a second, which three replicas handle. I emit a 302 so the browser comes back next time and I keep seeing clicks. The trade-off is the cache and DB can disagree for a moment, but mappings are immutable once created, so that's a non-issue."

> **🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
> **CDN** — a network of cache servers spread around the world that serve responses from near the user. Here it absorbs a chunk of redirect traffic before it ever reaches us.
> **Redis** — an in-memory key-value store with sub-millisecond reads. Here it holds the hot `code → URL` mappings so the DB is rarely touched.
> **Read replica** — a read-only copy of the database kept in sync with the primary. Here replicas serve the few cache misses so reads don't compete with writes.
> **302 Found** — an HTTP "temporary redirect" the browser doesn't permanently cache. Here it ensures every click comes back to us for analytics.

**Diagram — redirect (hot) flow with miss fallback**
```mermaid

    Client[Client app]:::client
    Client -->|"1: GET code"| CDN[CDN edge]:::ext
    CDN -->|"2: miss to origin"| RS[Redirect Service]:::svc
    RS -->|"3: read cache"| Redis[(Redis sharded)]:::db
    Redis -->|"4 hit: return URL"| RS
    RS -->|"5 miss: read replica"| Replica[(Postgres replica)]:::db
    Replica -->|"6: backfill cache"| Redis
    RS -->|"7: emit 302"| Client
    RS -.->|"8: fire click event"| Queue[[Click queue]]:::async
    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef async fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
